hypothesis: If open-weight language models for superalloy design are first supervised fine-tuned on composition–phase data and then optimized with DPO using Thermo-Calc–derived preference pairs, they will generate BCC/B2 compositions that more frequently meet the target phase-coexistence objectives (including BCC-first formation, room-temperature B2, and <10% secondary phases) with higher validity and novelty than SFT-only and other baselines because preference learning aligns generation with physics-based feedback while limiting distribution shift.
